mnemonics: DICKENS' ARIMA: Differencing Is Crucial, Keep Eliminating Non-Stationary Steps to Achieve Results, Invert to Model
keyPoints: A non-stationary time series can often be transformed into a stationary one by differencing, which is the foundation of the ARIMA model.
The order of differencing (d) in an ARIMA(p,d,q) model is determined by how many times the series must be differenced to achieve stationarity.
Over-differencing a series can introduce unnecessary structure and noise, making the model less effective, while under-differencing fails to address the non-stationarity.
commonMistakes: Failing to test for stationarity after each round of differencing, leading to an incorrect 'd' value.
Using an ARMA model on a non-stationary series without first applying differencing.
Misinterpreting a random walk as a stationary process because its changes are stationary, while the level itself is not.
